title: Easy Homemade Peach Galette with Buttery Flaky Crust Perfect for Summer

description: This peach galette is a simple, rustic dessert that’s full of summer sweetness. The flaky buttery crust pairs perfectly with juicy peaches and a touch of sugar. It’s my go-to for easy weekend baking when I want something homemade but not fussy.

pin_variations.title: Why Does This Peach Galette Taste So Much Better Than Store-Bought?
pin_variations.description: Discover the secret to a flaky crust and perfectly caramelized peaches 🍑. This galette is simple but packed with flavor and texture. Save this for your next dessert craving! #peachgalette #homemadedessert #summerbaking

pin_variations.title: The One Trick That Makes Homemade Peach Galette Foolproof Every Time
pin_variations.description: It’s all about chilling the dough and slicing peaches just right! This trick gives you a buttery crust and juicy filling every time. Try it out and tap for the full recipe 🍽️ #easybaking #peachgalette #summerdessert

pin_variations.title: 30-Minute Peach Galette Recipe Perfect for Busy Summer Evenings
pin_variations.description: Quick and easy, this peach galette comes together fast but tastes like you spent hours. Perfect for a last-minute dessert or an afternoon treat. Save this recipe for your next sweet fix! 🍑 #quickdessert #peachgalette #homemade

pin_variations.title: Peach Galette vs Peach Pie – Which Rustic Dessert Wins Your Heart?
pin_variations.description: Love a rustic dessert? See why this open-faced galette might just beat the classic pie with its flaky edges and juicy peaches. Save this recipe to try both! #peachgalette #dessertdebate #homemadebaking

ingredients: 8 tablespoons unsalted butter, cold and cubed
introduction: I first made this peach galette a few summers ago when I had a basket of fresh peaches from the farmer’s market and wanted something simple, not too sweet, and easy to put together. I love that this recipe skips the fuss of a pie pan and lattice crust but still delivers all the buttery, flaky goodness I crave. What makes this galette stand out is the balance between the tender, juicy peaches and the crisp, golden crust that’s just slightly caramelized on the edges. I usually chill my dough well and slice the peaches thinly to get that perfect tender filling that isn’t too runny. It’s the kind of dessert you can whip up on a weekend afternoon, and it’s always a hit with my family and friends. The rustic look means it doesn’t have to be perfect, which is a relief when you’re baking at home. Once baked, it has this warm, inviting aroma and a texture that’s flaky, juicy, and a little crisp all at once. I hope you enjoy making it as much as I do!

instructions.instruction: In a large bowl, whisk together the flour, salt, and sugar. Add the cold, cubed butter and use a pastry cutter or your fingers to work the butter into the flour until the mixture resembles coarse crumbs with some pea-sized pieces. This step is crucial for creating a flaky crust because the small chunks of butter melt during baking, creating steam pockets. Avoid overworking the dough to keep it tender.
instructions.doneness_cue: The dough should look crumbly with visible chunks of butter and no large clumps.

instructions.instruction: Slowly add ice water, one tablespoon at a time, mixing gently until the dough just comes together. You want it to hold when pressed but not be sticky. Too much water can make the dough tough. Form the dough into a disk, wrap in plastic wrap, and chill for at least 1 hour. Chilling firms the butter and relaxes the gluten, which helps the crust be tender and flaky.
instructions.doneness_cue: The dough should be firm and cold to the touch, easy to roll out without cracking.

instructions.instruction: While the dough chills, prepare the peach filling. In a large bowl, toss the sliced peaches with sugar, cornstarch, lemon juice, and cinnamon. The cornstarch helps thicken the juices released during baking, preventing a soggy crust. The lemon juice balances the sweetness and brightens the flavor. Let the peaches sit while you roll out the dough.
instructions.doneness_cue: The peaches should be evenly coated and slightly juicy but not watery.

instructions.instruction: Preheat the oven to 400°F (200°C). On a lightly floured surface, roll out the chilled dough into a 12-inch circle about 1/8 inch thick. Keep the edges slightly thicker for folding. Rolling evenly ensures uniform baking and a crisp crust. If the dough cracks at the edges, press it gently to seal.
instructions.doneness_cue: The dough should be smooth and even in thickness, about 12 inches in diameter.

instructions.instruction: Transfer the dough to a parchment-lined baking sheet. Spoon the peach mixture into the center, leaving a 2-inch border. Fold the edges of the dough up over the peaches in pleats, overlapping as needed. This rustic folding keeps the filling contained while allowing steam to escape. The free edges will crisp nicely in the oven.
instructions.doneness_cue: The edges should be folded neatly over the filling with no gaps.

instructions.instruction: Brush the crust edges with beaten egg and sprinkle coarse sugar on top. The egg wash promotes a golden, shiny crust, and the sugar adds a nice crunch and sparkle. This finishing touch makes the galette look irresistible and adds texture.
instructions.doneness_cue: The crust edges should be evenly coated and glistening.

instructions.instruction: Bake the galette in the preheated oven for 30-35 minutes until the crust is golden brown and the peach filling is bubbling. Keep an eye on the crust to avoid burning; if it browns too quickly, tent with foil. The bubbling filling indicates the peaches are cooked through and thickened.
instructions.doneness_cue: The crust should be deep golden and the filling visibly bubbling.

instructions.instruction: Remove the galette from the oven and let it cool on the baking sheet for at least 15 minutes. Cooling allows the filling to set slightly for easier slicing and prevents burns from hot juices. It also helps the crust crisp up as it cools.
instructions.doneness_cue: The filling should be thickened and the galette slightly cooled but still warm.

instructions.instruction: Serve warm or at room temperature, optionally with a scoop of vanilla ice cream or a dollop of whipped cream. The contrast of warm, flaky crust and cold ice cream is heavenly. Enjoy immediately for best texture and flavor.
instructions.doneness_cue: The galette should be warm with the filling tender and crust crisp.

why_this_recipe_works.section: Why This Peach Galette Recipe Works So Well
why_this_recipe_works.content:
This peach galette recipe succeeds because of the careful balance between a tender, flaky crust and a juicy, flavorful filling. The crust is made with cold butter cut into flour, which creates layers that puff and flake during baking. Chilling the dough before rolling is essential because it firms up the butter and relaxes the gluten, preventing toughness and shrinkage.

The filling uses fresh ripe peaches tossed with sugar, cornstarch, lemon juice, and cinnamon. The cornstarch thickens the natural juices that release during baking, preventing the crust from becoming soggy. Lemon juice brightens the flavor and balances the sweetness, while cinnamon adds a warm spice note that complements the peaches perfectly.

The rustic folding technique of the galette crust allows steam to escape, which helps the filling thicken and the crust to crisp nicely. Brushing the crust with egg wash and sprinkling coarse sugar creates a beautiful golden sheen and a pleasant crunch. Baking at a moderately high temperature ensures the crust browns evenly and the filling bubbles through the open top.

Overall, this recipe is approachable but delivers a classic French-inspired dessert with a flaky, buttery crust and luscious peach filling that’s perfect for summer gatherings or a simple family treat.

ingredient_notes.all-purpose flour: Choose a good quality all-purpose flour with moderate protein content (around 10-12%). It provides enough gluten for structure but keeps the crust tender. Avoid bread flour which can make the crust tough. Sifting the flour before mixing helps avoid lumps and ensures even distribution of ingredients.
ingredient_notes.unsalted butter: Use cold, unsalted butter for best control over salt and flavor. The cold butter creates steam pockets during baking which contribute to a flaky crust. Cubing the butter small but not too fine helps create layers. Avoid margarine or spreads as they don’t have the same fat content or melting properties.
ingredient_notes.peaches: Choose ripe but firm peaches for the best texture and flavor. Overripe peaches can become mushy and overly juicy, making the filling runny. Peeling the peaches is recommended for a smoother texture and prettier presentation. If peaches aren’t in season, frozen sliced peaches (thawed and drained) can be substituted.
ingredient_notes.cornstarch: Cornstarch acts as a thickening agent, absorbing the juices released by the peaches during baking. It prevents the filling from becoming watery and helps it set nicely. Avoid overusing cornstarch, as too much can give a gummy texture. Mix thoroughly with the peaches to avoid clumps.
ingredient_notes.egg wash: Beaten egg brushed on the crust before baking promotes browning and gives a shiny, appetizing finish. You can use whole egg or just egg yolk for more color. Skip this step if you want a more rustic matte finish, but the crust won’t brown as evenly.

pro_tips.tip: Keep your butter and water ice cold when making the dough.
pro_tips.reason: Cold butter creates steam pockets as it melts in the oven, which leads to a flaky crust. Warm butter blends too thoroughly with flour, resulting in a dense, tough pastry. Ice-cold water helps keep the dough cool and prevents gluten from developing too much.

pro_tips.tip: Don’t overmix the dough once you add water.
pro_tips.reason: Overmixing activates gluten, making the crust tough instead of tender. Mix just until the dough holds together. It’s okay if it’s a bit shaggy or crumbly; it will come together when you press it into a disk.

pro_tips.tip: Slice peaches evenly and not too thick.
pro_tips.reason: Even slices ensure the peaches cook uniformly, and thinner slices release less juice, preventing a soggy crust. Thick slices might stay undercooked or cause the crust to become soggy from excess moisture.

pro_tips.tip: Chill the dough for at least an hour before rolling.
pro_tips.reason: Chilling firms the butter and relaxes the gluten, which prevents shrinking and tough texture. It also makes the dough easier to roll out and fold without cracking.

pro_tips.tip: Use parchment paper to transfer and bake the galette.
pro_tips.reason: Parchment paper prevents sticking and makes cleanup easier. It also helps in sliding the galette onto the baking sheet without tearing the dough.

pro_tips.tip: Brush the crust with egg wash and sprinkle coarse sugar before baking.
pro_tips.reason: This gives the crust a beautiful golden color and a slight crunch, enhancing both appearance and texture.

pro_tips.tip: Let the galette cool slightly before slicing.
pro_tips.reason: Cooling allows the filling to set, making it easier to cut neat slices and preventing hot juices from spilling.

common_mistakes_to_avoid.mistake: Using warm butter or overworking the dough
common_mistakes_to_avoid.why_it_happens: Sometimes in a rush, people don’t chill butter enough or mix the dough too much, thinking it will help it come together better.
common_mistakes_to_avoid.fix: Always use cold butter and add water gradually, mixing gently until just combined. Chill the dough for at least an hour before rolling to keep the butter firm and the crust flaky.

common_mistakes_to_avoid.why_it_happens: Using overripe peaches or skipping the cornstarch causes excess juice to leak and make the crust soggy.
common_mistakes_to_avoid.fix: Use firm ripe peaches and toss them with cornstarch and sugar to absorb juices. Let the mixture sit for a few minutes before assembling to thicken.

common_mistakes_to_avoid.mistake: Crust burns before filling is cooked
common_mistakes_to_avoid.why_it_happens: Baking at too high a temperature or not monitoring the crust can cause edges to overbrown.
common_mistakes_to_avoid.fix: Bake at 400°F and tent the crust edges with foil halfway through if they brown too quickly.

common_mistakes_to_avoid.why_it_happens: Dough not chilled enough or too dry will crack when rolled out.
common_mistakes_to_avoid.fix: Chill dough thoroughly and add a teaspoon of water if it feels dry before rolling. Patch cracks gently with extra dough.

common_mistakes_to_avoid.why_it_happens: Cutting too soon or using a flimsy crust can cause it to crumble.
common_mistakes_to_avoid.fix: Let the galette cool for 15 minutes to set the filling and crust before slicing carefully with a sharp knife.

variations.name: Mixed Berry Galette
variations.description: Swap peaches for a combination of fresh berries like blueberries, raspberries, and strawberries. Adjust sugar slightly as berries can be more tart. The filling will be juicier, so add a bit more cornstarch to prevent sogginess.
variations.technical_note: Berries release more juice than peaches, so careful thickening with cornstarch and gentle folding of the crust edges is important to keep the galette intact.

variations.name: Peach and Almond Galette
variations.description: Add sliced almonds to the peach filling or sprinkle almond flour on the crust before folding. This adds a lovely nutty flavor and extra texture. You can also brush the crust with almond extract for a subtle aroma.
variations.technical_note: Almond flour can absorb some moisture from the filling, helping prevent soggy crust while adding flavor.

variations.name: Gluten-Free Peach Galette
variations.description: Use a gluten-free flour blend designed for baking in place of all-purpose flour. The texture will be slightly different but still delicious. Chill the dough well and handle gently to avoid crumbling.
variations.technical_note: Gluten-free doughs often need more careful handling and chilling to mimic the elasticity gluten provides.

variations.name: Vegan Peach Galette
variations.description: Replace butter with a vegan butter substitute and use a flax egg or aquafaba for the egg wash. The crust will still be flaky and delicious, and the filling remains the same.
variations.technical_note: Vegan butter should be cold and solid for best flakiness. Egg wash can be substituted with plant milk brushed on for color.

variations.name: Spiced Peach Galette
variations.description: Add warming spices like nutmeg, ginger, or cardamom to the peach filling for a more complex flavor. This is great for cooler months or when you want a cozy twist on summer fruit.
variations.technical_note: Use spices sparingly to complement, not overpower, the natural peach flavor.

nutrition.note: Nutritional values are estimates and may vary based on specific ingredients used.

storage_and_reheating.room_temperature: Store leftover peach galette covered loosely at room temperature for up to 1 day. Keep away from direct heat or sunlight to maintain crust texture. Best eaten within a day for optimal freshness.
storage_and_reheating.refrigerator: Place galette in an airtight container or cover with plastic wrap and refrigerate for up to 3 days. The crust may soften slightly but reheating in a warm oven can restore crispness.
storage_and_reheating.freezer: Wrap the galette tightly in plastic wrap and then foil to freeze for up to 2 months. Thaw overnight in the refrigerator before reheating. Freezing may slightly affect crust texture but flavor remains good.
storage_and_reheating.reheating: Reheat slices in a 350°F oven for 10-15 minutes to crisp the crust and warm the filling. Avoid microwaving as it makes the crust soggy. A splash of water or brush of melted butter before reheating can refresh the crust.
storage_and_reheating.make_ahead_strategy: Prepare the dough up to 2 days ahead and keep wrapped in the refrigerator. Assemble the galette and bake on the day you want to serve for the freshest crust and best flavor. You can also slice peaches and mix the filling a few hours ahead and refrigerate.

faq.question: Can I use frozen peaches for this galette?
faq.answer: Yes, you can use frozen peaches if fresh ones aren’t available. Just make sure to thaw them completely and drain any excess juice before mixing with sugar and cornstarch. This helps prevent a soggy crust. Frozen peaches might be softer, so handle gently.

faq.question: How do I prevent the crust from getting soggy?
faq.answer: The key is using cornstarch in the filling to thicken the juices and chilling the dough well before baking. Also, folding the crust edges up helps contain the filling and allows steam to escape. Baking at a higher temperature crisps the crust quickly, reducing sogginess.

faq.question: Can I make the dough in advance?
faq.answer: Absolutely! The dough can be made and refrigerated for up to 2 days before rolling out. Just keep it tightly wrapped in plastic wrap to prevent drying out. Bring it to the fridge when ready to use and roll out cold for best results.

faq.question: What if my dough cracks when rolling?
faq.answer: If the dough cracks, it likely needs more moisture or chilling. You can patch cracks by pressing the dough gently together or adding a teaspoon of ice water to help it hold. Always roll gently and keep the dough chilled.

faq.question: Can I substitute the butter with shortening or margarine?
faq.answer: Butter is preferred for flavor and texture, but you can use shortening or margarine. Keep in mind these fats have different melting points and water content, which may affect flakiness and taste. Using a mix of butter and shortening can be a good compromise.

faq.question: How do I store leftover galette?
faq.answer: Store leftovers in an airtight container at room temperature for a day or in the fridge for up to 3 days. Reheat in the oven to refresh the crust. Avoid microwaving as it makes the crust soggy.

faq.question: Can I add spices to the peach filling?
faq.answer: Yes, adding warm spices like cinnamon, nutmeg, or ginger can enhance the flavor. Use them sparingly so they complement rather than overpower the peaches. This is especially nice in cooler months.

faq.question: Is it necessary to peel the peaches?
faq.answer: Peeling peaches is recommended because the skin can be tough and affect the texture of the filling. If you prefer, you can leave the skin on, especially if peaches are very ripe and tender, but the texture will be less smooth.

conclusion: I hope you enjoy this easy peach galette as much as my family does. It’s a simple, delicious way to celebrate fresh fruit and homemade baking. Let me know how yours turns out!
